extension.valueMarkdown:
## Character
21-year-old African American male, junior at University of Wisconsin-Madison studying computer science. Lives in a dorm on campus. Generally healthy, no chronic conditions. Immunization history: received MMR at 12 months but did NOT receive the recommended second dose at 4-6 years (parents were vaccine-hesitant during that period, though they later resumed standard vaccinations). Insurance: parent's employer plan (Anthem).

## Clinical Scenario
In March 2026, Marcus returns from a spring break trip to the Philippines (visiting a friend's family in Manila) where a measles outbreak is ongoing. Five days after returning to campus, he develops high fever (104°F), cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis. He presents to UW Student Health Services thinking he has a bad flu. The clinician notes Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa and a maculopapular rash beginning on the face and spreading to the trunk.

Measles is suspected immediately. Marcus is isolated, and the case is reported to the Wisconsin Department of Health Services (DHS) and Public Health Madison & Dane County. Measles IgM serology and measles RNA PCR are sent — both return positive. He is confirmed as a measles case.

The public health investigation reveals that Marcus attended 3 days of classes (large lecture halls, 200+ students), ate in the dining hall, and used the campus gym during his infectious period (4 days before rash onset through 4 days after). This triggers a massive contact tracing effort and campus-wide exposure notification.

The Wisconsin DHS uses a Permission Ticket to access Marcus's clinical records from the university health center for the public health investigation.

## Sites
- **UW Health University Student Health Services** — Student health clinic in Madison, WI (2023–present). Routine visits, STI screening, the index measles visit, and follow-up.
- **Public Health Madison & Dane County / Wisconsin DHS** — Public health agency. Not a clinical site but the investigation authority that receives the case report.

One clinical site (plus the PH agency context). Tests Permission Ticket constraints on **scopes** (PH needs Condition, DiagnosticReport, Observation, Immunization, Encounter — not unrelated student health visits) and **periods** (just the exposure investigation window).

note.text: Pre-existing, mild. Newly documented at this encounter. Well controlled with OTC loratadine as needed.

code.text: Incomplete immunization status — incomplete MMR vaccination series

note.text: Single MMR dose at 12 months (June 2005), missing recommended second dose. Documented as incomplete MMR series — consider catch-up. Not flagged for immediate follow-up action at this visit.
2026-03-30: Resolved in the context of measles. Natural measles infection now confers durable immunity; second MMR dose no longer indicated for measles. Other immunizations remain up to date.

code.text: Encounter for routine screening for sexually transmitted infections

note.text: 5-lb weight loss from decreased oral intake during febrile illness.

Confirmed by positive IgM serology and positive RNA PCR. Classic presentation with Koplik spots, cough-coryza-conjunctivitis prodrome, and cephalocaudal maculopapular rash. Exposure likely during travel to Manila, Philippines (March 7-15, 2026). Patient had incomplete MMR vaccination (single dose at 12 months). State case ID: WI-MEAS-2026-0047.
2026-03-30: Clinical and laboratory recovery confirmed. No complications identified. Patient cleared to return to normal activities. Case reported as resolved to Wisconsin DHS.

dosageInstruction.text: 200,000 IU by mouth, one dose on day 1 (March 23) and one dose on day 2 (March 24) per WHO recommendation for measles
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 200,000 IU by mouth once today and once tomorrow (2 doses total).

note.text: WHO-recommended vitamin A supplementation for measles in young adults to reduce morbidity.

extension.valueMarkdown: Marcus presented for his initial student health visit upon enrollment at UW–Madison. This was a new patient encounter; records from his Milwaukee pediatrician were not fully transferred.

extension.valueMarkdown: Marcus presented with a 3-day history of nasal congestion, sore throat, and mild cough. He reported no fever at home but felt generally unwell.

location.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

serviceProvider.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

extension.valueMarkdown: Marcus requested routine STI screening. He reported one new sexual partner in the past 6 months, consistent condom use, no urinary symptoms, no genital lesions, no discharge.

location.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

serviceProvider.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

extension.valueMarkdown: Marcus presented urgently with a 3-day prodrome of malaise, loss of appetite, worsening cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis beginning March 20, followed by high fever and a spreading maculopapular rash that started on his face on March 22–23 and was progressing cephalocaudally to neck and trunk. He reported recent travel to Manila, Philippines (March 7–15, 2026), an area with active measles outbreak activity.

location.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

serviceProvider.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

extension.valueMarkdown: Scheduled telehealth video visit to assess Marcus during his isolation period (day 3 post-rash onset). Marcus joined the video call from his university isolation housing room.

location.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

serviceProvider.display: UW Health University Student Health Services

extension.valueMarkdown: Marcus returned for an in-person clearance visit following completion of his measles isolation period. He reported feeling significantly better — fever had resolved by March 28, appetite returning, energy improving.

resource.conclusion: Rapid strep test negative, supporting viral etiology.

coding.display: Sexually transmitted pathogens panel - Specimen by NAA with probe detection

resource.conclusion: STI screening panel — all results negative. Chlamydia NAAT negative, gonorrhea NAAT negative, HIV antigen/antibody screen non-reactive.

coding.display: CBC panel - Blood by Automated count

resource.conclusion: Leukopenia with lymphopenia consistent with acute measles infection.

coding.display: Comprehensive metabolic 2000 panel - Serum or Plasma

resource.conclusion: Mildly elevated AST; otherwise within normal limits.

resource.conclusion: Measles IgM antibody positive, confirming acute measles infection.

resource.conclusion: Leukocyte count recovered, lymphopenia resolved. All values within normal limits.

resource.conclusion: CRP significantly decreased from 48 mg/L to 8 mg/L, consistent with resolving inflammation.
